coredata.dc:title: Graphene-quantum-dots-induced MnO2 with needle-like nanostructure grown on carbonized wood as advanced electrode for supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Weiye
Yang, Yingni
Xia, Rongqi
Li, Yanchen
Zhao, Junqi
Lin, Lin
Cao, Jiaming
Wang, Qihang
Liu, Yi
Guo, Hongwu
coredata.dc:description: Hydrothermal and electrochemical deposition methods have been employed to fabricate porous wood carbon (PWC)/pseudocapacitive hybrid materials for use as free-standing supercapacitor electrodes. However, their cycling stability is poor because of the inherent storage mechanism of pseudocapacitive materials, and their specific capacitance requires further improvement. In this study, PWC was directly produced as a conductive matrix by pyrolyzing natural balsa wood, and then manganese dioxide (MnO2) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were deposited to fabricate a PWC/MnO2/GQDs electrode by a hydrothermal method. GQDs significantly boosts the ions transport, and protects MnO2 from falling off both the external surface and inside the channel of PWC. Compared with a PWC/MnO2 electrode, the unique needle-like nanostructures formed by adding GQDs resulted in a better electrochemical performance for a supercapacitor electrode, including a moderate areal specific capacitance (2712 mF cm−2 at a current density of 1.0 mA cm−2), good rate capability, and excellent cycling stability (95.3% retention after 2000 cycles). This indicated that GQDs-decorated composites will promote the development of high-performance energy storage devices.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of porous wood carbon/MnO2/GQDs (PWC/MnO2/GQDs) composite

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
Natural Science Foundation of Beijing Municipality

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the synthesis of the PWC/MnO2 and PWC/MnO2/GQDs electrodes. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Representative photograph of PWC. SEM images of (b) top view of PWC (c–d) magnification of b, (e) top view of PWC/MnO2 and (f) magnification of e. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
SEM images of PWC/MnO2/GQDs using different GQDs contents (a–b) 5 mg, (c–d) 10 mg, (e–f) 15 mg, and (g–h) 20 mg. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) GCD curves for various GQDs contents at the same current density of 1 mA cm−2; (b) the corresponding areal capacitances and specific capacitances for various GQDs contents at the same current density of 1 mA cm−2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) SEM images of PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg composites, (b–d) Element mapping images of C, O, and Mn. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a–b) TEM images and (c–d) HRTEM images of the PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg composites; inset in (d) shows the SAED pattern of the PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg composite. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) XRD patterns of the PWC, PWC/MnO2, and PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg composites; (b) Raman spectra of the PWC, PWC/MnO2, and PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg composites; (c) survey XPS spectra of PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg composites; and (d) Mn 2p core-level XPS spectra of PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg composites. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a–c) Electrochemical performance of PWC/MnO2: (a) CV curves at various scan rates, (b) GCD curves at different current densities, and (c) rate performances. (d–f) Electrochemical performances of PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg: (d) CV curves at various scan rates, (e) GCD curves at different current densities, and (f) rate performances. (g) impedance curve of PWC/MnO2 and PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg electrodes, (h) cycling performance of PWC/MnO2, and (i) cycling performance of PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic illustration of ions and charge transfer inside the PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg electrode. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Graphene-quantum-dots-induced MnO2 with needle-like nanostructure grown on carbonized wood as advanced electrode for supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of Wooden Material Science and Application, Beijing Forestry University, Beijing, 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory of Wooden Material Science and Application

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . MOE Key Laboratory of Wooden Material Science and Application, Beijing Forestry University, Beijing, 100083, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Material Science and Engineering, Beihua University, Jilin, 132000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . College of Material Science and Engineering, Beihua University, Jilin, 132000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrothermal and electrochemical deposition methods have been employed to fabricate porous wood carbon (PWC)/pseudocapacitive hybrid materials for use as free-standing supercapacitor electrodes. However, their cycling stability is poor because of the inherent storage mechanism of pseudocapacitive materials, and their specific capacitance requires further improvement. In this study, PWC was directly produced as a conductive matrix by pyrolyzing natural balsa wood, and then manganese dioxide (MnO2) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were deposited to fabricate a PWC/MnO2/GQDs electrode by a hydrothermal method. GQDs significantly boosts the ions transport, and protects MnO2 from falling off both the external surface and inside the channel of PWC. Compared with a PWC/MnO2 electrode, the unique needle-like nanostructures formed by adding GQDs resulted in a better electrochemical performance for a supercapacitor electrode, including a moderate areal specific capacitance (2712 mF cm−2 at a current density of 1.0 mA cm−2), good rate capability, and excellent cycling stability (95.3% retention after 2000 cycles). This indicated that GQDs-decorated composites will promote the development of high-performance energy storage devices.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid growth of the global energy demand, efficient energy storage has become particularly critical [1,2]. An electrochemical energy storage device is a typical high-efficiency energy storage system (e.g., supercapacitors [3], lithium-ion batteries [4], and lithium-air batteries [5,6]). Among these, supercapacitors have attracted much attention due to their high power density, fast charge-discharge properties, and long cycle life compared with other energy storage devices [7–11]. Developing favorable electrode materials is an effective way to fabricate high performance supercapacitors [12]; for instance, the usual large-sized carbonaceous materials such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphene sheets possess high power density and cycling stability [13]. However, these must be mixed with adhesives and conductive additives to form pastes during electrode preparation and then coated on collectors to evaluate their electrochemical properties [14]. The addition of additives increases the charge transport resistance, which is not condutive to improving the specific capacitance of the electrode [15,16].

Wood has a unique aligned structure and is bio-based, renewable, and sustainable [17]. Porous wood carbon (PWC) has straight channels, which are ideally suited to fast mass transport along the channel directions, while maintaining mechanical consistency, so it is a perfect choice for constructing a high-performance electrode [18,19]. Pure PWC supercapacitors (with non-faradaic electric double-layer capacitance) show poor electrochemical activity, and the corresponding specific capacitances are generally only several tens of farads per gram [20]. When using anisotropic block structures of wood carbon materials and pseudocapacitive filling materials in wood cells, the hybrid supercapacitors show significantly improved electrochemical properties [21,22]. Among various pseudocapacitive materials, manganese dioxide (MnO2) has attracted tremendous attention due to its low cost, non-toxicity, and high specific capacitance (1370 F g−1 per manganese atom) [23]; these MnO2 nanostructures must be grown or supported on highly porous and electrically conductive matrices to form composite materials to enhance their electrochemical performance [24]. However, adding MnO2 decreases the charging and discharging speeds, reducing the stability of the device [25]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a zero-dimensional graphene material have also received considerable interest due to their unique optical and electrochemical properties [26]. Li et al. reported three-dimensional (3D) CNT/carbon cloth network/GQDs (CNT/CC/GQDs) hybrid all-solid-state flexible supercapacitors; the obtained CNT/CC/GQDs electrodes for all-solid-state flexible supercapacitors exhibited an ultra-high areal capacitance reaching 461 mF cm−2 at a current density of 0.5 mA cm−2 [27]. Huang and co-workers synthesized a GQDs/NiCo2S4 composite electrode, which exhibited high specific capacitance (678.22 F g−1 at 0.2 A g−1) and remarkable cycling stability (94% retention after 5000 cycles) [28]. These studies show that GQDs have excellent potential for enhancing the specific capacitance, rate performance, and cycling stability for supercapacitors [29]. In this context, it is necessary to study the role of GQDs in electrode [30].

Herein, we directly used PWC obtained from pyrolysis of natural balsa wood as a 3D highly conductive substrate; MnO2 and GQDs were homogeneously deposited on PWC frames by a one-step hydrothermal method, so that unique needle-like nanostructures provided fast 3D pathways for both electron transport and electrolyte penetration/diffusion. The newly synthesized PWC/MnO2/GQDs nanocomposites were applied as supercapacitor electrodes, and their electrochemical performance was evaluated by comparing it with that of the PWC/MnO2 electrode. The as prepared PWC/MnO2/GQDs electrode demonstrated a moderate areal specific capacity (2712 mF cm−2 at a current density of 1.0 mA cm−2), an outstanding rate capability (1075 mF cm−2 at a current density of 20 mA cm−2), and excellent cycling stability with 95.3% retention over 2000 cycles. This enhanced performance of the PWC/MnO2/GQDs electrode is attributed mainly to the porous 3D structure of wood carbon and needle-like nanostructures formed by adding GQDs.

Natural balsa wood was cut into slices with dimensions 2 cm × 2 cm × 0.1 cm (longitudinal × tangential × radial). These wood samples were washed repeatedly with distilled water and then dried at 60 °C for 24 h in a vacuum. GQDs were obtained from Suzhou Hengqiu Graphene Technology Co., Ltd. (Jiangsu, China). Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) and sodium sulfate (Na2SO4) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the experiments.

The wood carbon was fabricated by placing the wood samples into a tube furnace under the protection of nitrogen. The heating rate was set as 5 °C·min−1, the wood slice was firstly stabilized 500 °C for 1 h, and then the temperature was increased to 1000 °C and carbonized for 2 h. Finally, the temperature was decreased naturally to the room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of porous wood carbon/MnO2/GQDs (PWC/MnO2/GQDs) composite
ce:section.ce:para: The PWC/MnO2/GQDs composite was synthesized by a simple hydrothermal process. Briefly, the GQDs (15 mg) were added to 50 mL of the mixed solution containing 0.1 M KMnO4 and 0.1 M Na2SO4. Afterwards, the solution was stirred with a magnetic bar at room temperature for 8 h. A clean wood carbon slice was dipped in a mixed solution, then the mixed solution with the precursors was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and kept in an oven at 150 °C for 1.5 h; the sample was then washed with DI water and finally dried at 60 °C for 24 h in a vacuum. To investigate the effect of the GQDs content in the composite electrode materials, other composite materials with different GQDs contents (0, 5, 10, and 20 mg) were prepared.

The morphologies of the slices were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL, JSM-7001F) equipped with energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, TF20). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) were performed with a JEOL JSM-2100F microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed on a Rigaku Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα (λ = 1.54178 Å) radiation over a scan range of 10°–80°. Raman spectra were recorded at 633 nm excitation with a Raman spectrometer (LabRam HR Evolution). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI spectrometer to analyze the chemical composition of the slices.

All electrochemical performances were evaluated by using a three-electrode test cell with a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co.) in 1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte. In order to facilitate the electrochemical calculation, PWC/MnO2 and PWC/MnO2/GQDs were cut on one side, and the size of the electrode after cutting is 1.5 × 1 × 0.09 cm. Then, the top of the electrode is clamped, so the dimension of each working electrode was 1 × 1 × 0.09 cm (Figs. S1e and f). Pt foil and Ag/AgCl electrodes served as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) curves were obtained in a voltage window from 0 to 0.8 V. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) spectra were obtained in a frequency range of 105-10−2 Hz. For the PWC/MnO2/GQDs electrodes, the areal specific capacitances (Cs) were calculated from GCD curves based on the following equation:
(1)
C
s
= I Δ t / s Δ V
or
C
m
= I Δ t / m Δ V

Here, I (A) is the discharge current, Δt (s) is the discharge time, ΔV (V) is the potential window, s (cm2) is the area of the electrode exposed to the electrolyte and m (g) is the total mass of electrodes.

Fig. 1 illustrates the preparation of the PWC/MnO2 and PWC/MnO2/GQDs electrodes. Briefly, natural balsa wood was cut into slices along the growth direction to retain the highly porous structure, and then the wood slices were carbonized in a horizontal tube furnace under nitrogen to obtain a 3D aligned porous carbon matrix. MnO2 was then grown in situ both on the surface of the PWC and inside its channels by a hydrothermal method. To study the influence of the GQDs on the electrochemical performance of the electrode, the PWC/MnO2/GQDs electrode, was fabricated by adding GQDs to KMnO4 solution.

Balsa wood (Ochroma pyramidale), with a low density, many internal pores, and few extracts, is usually used as a model material. In recent years, it has been used in energy storage [31,32], sewage treatment [33], and structural materials [34–36]. By carbonizing the natural balsa wood to obtain PWC, its natural structural advantage is conducive to the loading of more active materials per unit mass, which is beneficial to the construction of thick electrodes [37]. Fig. 2 a shows a representative photograph of the PWC; the prepared electrode had an area of 2.25 cm2 (1.5 × 1.5 cm2) and a thickness of 0.09 cm (Fig. S1d). Compared with natural wood (Figs. S1a and b), PWC shows the phenomenon of size reduction and distortion. Size reduction can be explained by the loss of absorbed water during wood pyrolysis and the degradation of hemicellulose, cellulose and lignin. While distortion phenomenon resulted from nonuniform shrinkage due to the orientation of grain [38]. A top-view SEM image of PWC is shown in Fig. 2b; PWC maintained the features of natural balsa wood after carbonization but had a different pore structure (large channels (30–50 μm) and numerous small channels (2–4 μm)). Nanopores were observed inside the channels at higher magnification (Fig. 2c). PWC was then immersed in KMnO4 solution for 1.5 h after hydrothermal treatment, resulting in the following redox reaction between carbon and MnO4 − [39]:
4 M n
O
4
−
+ 3 C +
H
2
O → 4 M n
O
2
+ C
O
2
+ H C
O
3
−

On the initial smooth surface of PWC (Fig. 2d), a homogeneous layer of MnO2 nanoparticles (Fig. 2e) was deposited. A higher magnification SEM image in Fig. 2f shows MnO2 nanoparticles growing inside the PWC channels.

Fig. 3 a–h show top-view SEM images of PWC/MnO2/GQDs prepared with various GQDs contents. After adding the GQDs to KMnO4 solution, the smooth surface of the MnO2 particles appears to bulge (Fig. 3a and b). On adding GQDs, these bumps further differentiate and grow (Fig. 3c and d). When the dosage reaches 15 mg, a unique needle-like nanostructure is formed on the surface and inside the PWC (Fig. 3e and f). However, when the dosage reaches 20 mg, the surface bulge of MnO2 disappears (Fig. 3g and h); it is assumed that this needle-like structure grows rapidly and finally collapses.

To study the influence of the electrode microstructure on the electrochemical performance, GCD curves of pristine PWC electrodes were firstly obtained for a three-electrode test system at the different current density. As shown in Fig. S2(b), when the PWC electrode is charged and discharged with a weak current of 0.1 mA cm−2, the areal capacitance is only 9 mF cm−2, and the corresponding specific capacitance is 1.125 F g−1, showing very poor electrochemical performance; When MnO2 was deposited, the areal capacitances increased to 1180 mF cm−2 and the specific capacitance upgraded to 98.3 F g−1 at the current density of 1 mA cm−2 (Fig. 4 b), indicating that PWC only played a role of porous conductive carbon framework, and the capacitance of PWC/MnO2 electrode almost derived from the pseudocapacitance provided by MnO2. Capacitive performance of PWC/MnO2 is further enhanced by adding the GQDs. GCD curves for various GQDs contents at the same current density of 1 mA cm−2 are compared in Fig. 4a, the corresponding areal capacitance and specific capacitance are shown in Fig. 4b, when the addition of GQDs is 5 mg and 10 mg, the capacitance performance is improved, the electrode with 15 mg GQDs had the best electrochemical performance, corresponding areal specific capacitances were calculated as 2712 mF cm−2. Based on the experimental results, the GQDs improved the specific capacitance of the electrode. When the GQDs content was 15 mg, the unique needle-like nanostructure dramatically accelerated the ion diffusion and electron transport to promote the complete redox reactions [40]. Consequently, the PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg electrode could achieve a significantly increased specific capacitance. However, adding excess GQDs led to the collapse of the needle-like nanostructure, which was not conductive to the storage of electrons and ions, resulting in a decrease in the specific capacitance.

The EDX mapping of the PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg electrode is presented in Fig. 5 . The mapping indicates that the C, O, and Mn existed in needle-like nanostructures. TEM images of the PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg composite are presented in Fig. 6 (a and b), which shows the in situ loaded MnO2 on the walls of PWC with no conspicuous agglomeration.

The crystal structure of the PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg composites was further characterized by HRTEM. Fig. 6d shows well-resolved lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.25 nm, which can be indexed to the (101) plane of birnessite-type MnO2. Fig. 6d shows several of GQDs (marked with red circles) attached to the surfaces of the MnO2 to form a hybrid network structure. Birnessite-type MnO2 is a layered structure containing many water molecules, so the anchoring of GQDs onto the surfaces of MnO2 is mainly ascribed to the strong hydrogen bonds between the oxygen-containing functional groups (carboxyl groups) on the GQDs (Fig. S3) and hydrous MnO2 (hydroxyl groups) [41]. The hydrous MnO2 can form charge-transfer interactions with oxygenated functional groups, therefore, GQDs can both effectively inhibit the agglomeration of MnO2 and even the formation of larger particles and facilitate the fast transfer of electrons and movement of ions during the charge-discharge process [42]. In addition, the lattice spacing is 0.343 nm, which is in excellent agreement with the (002) spacing of graphitic carbon [40]. The corresponding SAED pattern exhibits sharp diffraction rings and spots owing to the random orientation of the nanostructure (inset in Fig. 6d).

The crystal structure of the GQDs and MnO2 in the composites was identified by XRD. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the XRD pattern of PWC exhibited a strong broad peak located at about 2θ = 24° and a weak peak centered at around 43.9°, which were attributed to the (002) and (100) diffraction planes, respectively, of graphitic stacks [42]. For the PWC/MnO2 composites, the intensities of the diffraction peaks from PWC decreased, and several new peaks located at around 12.4°, 36.4°, and 65.6° were observed, which corresponded to the (001), (200), and (020) planes of the birnessite-type MnO2 crystalline phase (JCPDS no. 42–1317) [43], showing that MnO2 was successfully loaded on the surface and in the pores of PWC. For the PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg composites, a diffraction peak belonging to the (002) crystal plane was observed, indicating that the GQDs were doped in MnO2, in accordance with the HRTEM results.

Raman spectroscopy was conducted to further study the structural characteristics of the samples (Fig. 7b). The peaks at ∼1350 and ∼1580 cm−1 correspond to the D and G bands, respectively. The ID/IG intensity ratio represents the degree of disorder of the carbon materials [44]. The intensity ratio (ID/IG) calculated from the Raman spectrum was around 1, confirming the domination of disordered structure in material, which was similar to the previous reported carbon materials obtained by chemical activation agent [45]. To further confirm the chemical state of Mn in the PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg composite, XPS was performed. The XPS signals of C, Mn, and O are shown in Fig. 7c, in addition, the Na and K elements were also detected. The Na element is thought to come in part from Na2SO4, which can be used as mineralizer to promote the precipitation of MnO2 under hydrothermal conditions, the other part comes from GQDs (Fig. S3b). K signal might be derived from the KMnO4 since there is always a possibility of K+ co-existing in the MnO2 [20]. Fig. 7d shows a high-resolution XPS spectrum of Mn 2p, which features two peaks at 653.8 and 642.1 eV, with a spin-energy separation of 11.7 eV, indicating that the Mn oxidation state is Mn4+ (MnO2) [43].

To compare the performances of the PWC/MnO2 and PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg electrodes, the electrochemical properties were investigated in a three-electrode configuration with 1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution as electrolyte within the potential range of 0–0.8 V. The CV curves of the two electrodes exhibited a near rectangular shape at different scan rates, as shown in Fig. 8 a and d, the current density and the integrated area of the CV curve obtained from the PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg electrode were much larger than those for PWC/MnO2, indicating that the electrochemical performance was improved after adding the GQDs, as described in Ref. [46]. This can be attributed to the introduction of GQDs and the unique needle-like nanostructure. The capacitances of the PWC/MnO2 and PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg nanocomposite electrodes were estimated from GCD tests. The GCD curves of the PWC/MnO2 (Fig. 8b) and PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg electrodes (Fig. 8e) with a triangular and symmetric shape reveal the excellent electrochemical reversibility. The discharge areal and specific capacitances were calculated using equation (1), as plotted in Fig. 8c and f. For the PWC/MnO2 electrode, an areal capacitance of 1180 mF cm−2 was achieved at 1 mA cm−2; when the current density increased to 20 mA cm−2, the capacity decayed to 14.5 mF cm−2. The specific capacitances calculated based on the mass of the total mass of the PWC/MnO2 composite slowly decrease from a maximum value of 98.3 to 2.42 F g−1 as the current densities increase from 1 to 20 mA cm−2, indicating a poor rate capability. However, the PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg electrode exhibited moderate areal capacitances of 2,712, 2,495, 2,056, and 1637 mF cm−2 at 1, 2, 5, and 10 mA cm−2. The high corresponding mass specific capacitances based on mass are 188.4, 177.3, 142.8 and 113.7 F g−1, respectively. Even at a current density of 20 mF cm−2, an areal capacitance of 1075 mF cm−2 could be retained. The corresponding specific capacitance was 74.7 F g−1. Nevertheless, the areal specific capacitance of 2712 mF cm−2 in this work is relatively lower, as compared with that of some recently reported porous wood carbon (PWC)/pseudocapacitive hybrid materials, such as MnO2@CW (4155 mF cm−2) [47], Co(OH)2@CW (3723 mF cm−2) [48], the areal specific capacitance of PWC/MnO2/GQDs electrode is expected to be further improved by the following modifications: (1) By designing the electrode structure, while maintaining or improving the battery performance of the active material per unit mass or volume, the proportion of inactive components is minimized [49]. (2) A special oxidation of carbon for increasing the surface functionality (through chemical treatment, electrochemical polarization, plasma treatment [50]. In comparison with the PWC/MnO2 electrode, the improved rate capability of the PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg electrode can be attributed to the unique 3D needle-like structure and the effective combination of GQDs with MnO2. It is believed that the 3D needle-like nanostructure offers a larger specific surface area, which can improve the contact between the electrode and electrolyte. The combination of MnO2 and GQDs with different redox reaction potentials creates more electroactive sites for the faradaic redox reactions [51].

EIS was performed in a frequency range of 10−5 to 0.01 Hz to further evaluate the electrochemical behaviors of the PWC/MnO2 and PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg electrodes. The EIS data were analyzed using Nyquist plots (Fig. 8g). The Nyquist plots comprised the internal resistance (Rs), charge transport resistance (Rct), Warburg resistance (W), and double layer capacitance (C) [46]. Rs for the PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg electrode was measured as 5.72 Ω, which was much smaller than that of the PWC/MnO2 electrode (6.78 Ω). The PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg electrode also showed a lower Rct (0.4 Ω) than that of the PWC/MnO2 electrode (1.5 Ω). These results show that the PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg electrode has lower resistance and faster electron transfer than the PWC/MnO2 electrode.

In addition, the long-term cycling performances of the PWC/MnO2 and PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg electrodes were evaluated by repeating the charge-discharge measurements at a constant current density of 10 mA cm−2. The PWC/MnO2 electrode showed a slightly decreased capacitance retention of 87.4% of its initial capacitance after 2000 cycles. Conversely, the PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg electrode demonstrated good long-term cycling stability, retaining approximately 95.3% after 2000 cycles. The improved cycling stability can also be ascribed to both the PWC substrate and needle-like structure. First, the PWC can be used as a self-supporting conductive substrate, which reduces volume expansion and alleviates structural damage. Second, the unique needle-like nanostructure can relieve the structural damage caused by volume expansion during cycling.

The experimental results above indicate that the electrochemical performance of PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg is apparently higher than that of PWC/MnO2. The enhanced electrochemical performance of the PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg electrode maybe due to the synergic effect of three materials. As shown in Fig. 9 , the reasons are summarized as follows: (1) PWC is used as a self-supporting 3D conductive carbon framework, its unique straight path reduces the curvature of the electrode, enables rapid penetration of electrolyte, and effectively improves the transmission rate of electrons and ions. (2) Due to the high porosity, thin cell wall and few extractions of balsa wood, the high-quality loading of active materials can be realized after carbonization, which greatly improves the specific capacitance of the electrode. (3) Those vertically grown MnO2 on both the external surface and inside the channel of PWC offer more opened active sites for faradaic reactions. (4) GQDs are dispersed in MnO2 to form a mixed network structure, reducing the agglomeration of MnO2 nanoparticles. Thus the whole redox reaction is promoted. (5) Most important of all, the unique needle-like nanostructure can increase the contact area between the electrode and electrolyte, which is conducive to the rapid transport and transfer of electrons and ions.

In summary, a wood-based supercapacitor electrode was fabricated by hydrothermal loading of MnO2 and GQDs on PWC. Compared with the PWC/MnO2 electrode, introducing GQDs can affect the structure formation of MnO2, converting MnO2 nanoparticles into a needle-like nanostructure. Consequently, the PWC/MnO2/GQDs15mg electrode exhibited a moderate areal capacitance (2712 mF cm−2 at 1 mA cm−2), good rate capacitance, and enhanced cycling stability (95.3% retention after 2000 cycles). These results indicate that GQDs are a promising material for high-performance supercapacitor electrodes. This work provides a novel insight into the design of high-performance electrochemical energy storage devices.

Weiye Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Yingni Yang: Methodology, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Rongqi Xia: Writing - review & editing. Yanchen Li: Software. Junqi Zhao: Formal analysis. Lin Lin: Writing - review & editing. Jiaming Cao: Visualization. Qihang Wang: Resources. Yi Liu: Supervision. Hongwu Guo: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This word was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Beijing Municipality (6184045) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2018ZY12).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2020.02.039.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanocellulose toward advanced energy storage devices: structure and electrochemistry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.J. Chen, L.B. Hu, Nanocellulose toward Advanced Energy Storage Devices: Structure and Electrochemistry, Acc. Chem. Res 51 (12) (2018) 3154-3165.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Where do batteries end and supercapacitors begin?

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Simon, Y. Gogotsi, B. Dunn, Where do batteries end and supercapacitors begin? Science 343 (2014) 1210-1211.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cable-type supercapacitors of three-dimensional cotton thread based multi-grade nanostructures for wearable energy storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N.S. Liu, W.Z. M, J.Y. Tao, X.H. Zhang, J. Su, L.Y. Li, et al., Cable-type supercapacitors of three-dimensional cotton thread based multi-grade nanostructures for wearable energy storage, Adv. Mater. 25 (2013) 4925-4931.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-capacity, low-tortuosity, and channel-guided lithium metal anode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhang, W. Luo, C.W Wang, Y.J. Li, C.J. Chen, J.W. Song, et al., High-capacity, low-tortuosity, and channel-guided lithium metal anode, Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. USA 114 (2017) 3584-3589.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nature-inspired tri-pathway design enabling high-performance flexible Li–O2 batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.C. Chen, S.M. Xu, Y.D. Kuang, W.T. Gan, J.W. Song, G.G. Chen, et al., Nature-inspired tri-pathway design enabling high-performance flexible Li-O2 batteries, Adv. Energy. Mater. 9 (2019) 1802964-1802973.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Flexible lithium-CO2 battery with ultrahigh capacity and stable cycling

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.M. Xu, C.J. Chen, Y.D. Kuang, J.W. Song, W.T. Gan, B.Y. Liu, et al., Flexible lithium-CO2 battery with ultrahigh capacity and stable cycling, Energy Environ. Sci. 11 (2018) 3231-3237.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of flexible electrode based on cotton/polypyrrole/multi-walled carbon nanotube composite for supercapacitors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Yang, Y.P. Zhao, Z.S. Cai, B. Addie, C.H. Liu, K. Frank, Facile synthesis of flexible electrode based on cotton/polypyrrole/multi-walled carbon nanotube composite for supercapacitors, Cellulose 25 (2018) 4079-4091.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrathin MnO2 nanofibers grown on graphitic carbon spheres as high-performance asymmetric supercapacitor electrodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.B. Lei, J.T. Zhang, X. S. Zhao, Ultrathin MnO2 nanofibers grown on graphitic carbon spheres as high-performance asymmetric supercapacitor electrodes, J. Mater. Chem 22 (2012) 153-160.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly anisotropic, multichannel wood carbon with optimized heteroatom doping for supercapacitor and oxygen reduction reaction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.J. Tang, Z.X. Pei, Z.F. Wang, H.F. Li, J. Zeng, Z.H. Ruan, et al., Highly anisotropic, multichannel wood carbon with optimized heteroatom doping for supercapacitor and oxygen reduction reaction, Carbon 130 (2018) 532-543.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cu superstructures fabricated using tree leaves and Cu-MnO2 superstructures for high performance supercapacitors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Pang, S.M. Wang, G.C. Li, Y.H. Ma, J. Li, Y.H. Ma, et al,. Cu superstructures fabricated using tree leaves and Cu-MnO2 superstructures for high performance supercapacitors, J. Mater. Chem. A 1 (2013) 5053-5060.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Eco-eriendly wood-based solid-state flexible supercapacitors from wood transverse section slice and reduced graphene oxide

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.Y. Lv, F. Fu, S.Q. Wang, J.D. Huang, L. Hu, Eco-eriendly wood-based solid-state flexible supercapacitors from wood transverse section slice and reduced graphene oxide, Electron. Mater. Lett. 11 (4) (2015) 633-642.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Assembling nitrogen and oxygen co-doped graphene quantum dotsonto hierarchical carbon networks for all-solid-state flexible supercapacitors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.Li, Y.F. Li, L. Wang, L. Cao, X. Liu, Z.W Chen, et al., Assembling nitrogen and oxygen co-doped graphene quantum dotsonto hierarchical carbon networks for all-solid-state flexible supercapacitors, Electrochim. Acta 235 (2017) 561-569.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene-quantum-dots induced NiCo2S4 with hierarchical-like hollow nanostructure for supercapacitors with enhanced electrochemical performance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.Y. Huang, T.L. Shi, Y. Zhong , S.Y. Cheng , S.L. Jiang , C. Chen , et, al., Graphene-quantum-dots induced NiCo2S4 with hierarchical-like hollow nanostructure for supercapacitors with enhanced electrochemical performance, Electrochim. Acta 269 (2018) 45-54.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A high-performance, all-textile and spirally wound asymmetric supercapacitors based on core–sheath structured MnO2 nanoribbons and cotton-derived carbon cloth

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.C. Wan, Y. Jiao, D.X. Liang, Y.Q. Wu, J. Li, A high-performance, all-textile and spirally wound asymmetric supercapacitors based on core-sheath structured MnO2 nanoribbons and cotton-derived carbon cloth, Electrochim. Acta 285 (2018) 262-271.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly anisotropic, multichannel wood carbon with optimized heteroatom doping for supercapacitor and oxygen reduction reaction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.J. Tang, Z.X. Pei, Z.F. Wang, H.F. Li, J. Zeng, Z.H. R, et,al., Highly anisotropic, multichannel wood carbon with optimized heteroatom doping for supercapacitor and oxygen reduction reaction, Carbon 130 (2018) 532-543.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.C. Liu, L.B. Kong, P. Zhang, Y.C. Luo, L Kang, Porous wood carbon monolith for high-performance supercapacitors, Electrochim. Acta 60 (2012) 443-448.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.M. Wang, X.J. Lin, T. Liu, H. Chen, S. Chen, Z.J. Jiang, et,al., Wood-derived hierarchically porous electrodes for high-performance all-solid-state supercapacitors, Adv. Funct. Mater. 24 (2018) 1806207-1806217.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Manganese dioxide supported on porous biomorphic carbons as hybrid materials for energy storage devices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.G. Pardo, B. Lacroix, J.M. Fernandez, J.R. Rico, Manganese dioxide supported on porous biomorphic carbons as hybrid materials for energy storage devices, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 8 (2016) 30890-30898.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. C. Wan, Y. Jiao, J. Li, Core-shell composite of wood-derived biochar supported MnO2 nanosheets for supercapacitor applications, RSC. Adv 6 (69) (2016) 64811-64817.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhang, C.L. Wu, W. Wu, C. Zhou, Z.W. Xi, Y.Y. Deng, et,al., High performance flexible supercapacitors based on porous wood carbon slices derived from Chinese fir wood scraps, J. Power Sources 424 (2019) 1-7.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.E. Fischer, M.P. Saunders, K.A. Pettigrew, D.R. Rolison, J. W. Long, Electroless deposition of nanoscale MnO2 on ultraporous carbon nanoarchitectures: correlation of evolving pore-solid structure and electrochemical performance, J. Electrochem. Soc. 155 (3) (2008).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhang, C.L. Wu, W. Wu, C. Zhou, Z.W. Xi, Y.Y. Deng, et,al., High performance flexible supercapacitors based on porous wood carbon slices derived from Chinese fir wood scraps, J. Power Sources 424 (2019) 1-7.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.Y. Ma, W.Y. Yuan, Y.H. Bai, H. Wu, L.F. Cheng, et al., The toughening design of pseudocapacitive materials via graphene quantum dots: Towards enhanced cycling stability for supercapacitors, Carbon 154 (2019) 292-300.
